exec.title: ANSR Named Leader in 2026 ISG Provider Lens for GCC Services
exec.body: ANSR was recognised as a Leader in the 2026 ISG Provider Lens™ for Global Capability Center Services, validating its end-to-end GCC design, build, and operate model across 225+ centers. The firm has hired 250,000+ professionals and manages 14 million sq ft of workspace in India. ISG analyst Gaurang Pagdi cited ANSR's subscription-based infrastructure model and full-stack ownership as key differentiators enabling enterprises to launch GCCs with minimal friction.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: As GCC buildout complexity rises, managed-service and platform-led models are becoming the benchmark—GCC leaders should benchmark their own setup frameworks against these emerging industry standards to accelerate time-to-capability.

exec.title: CGI Elevate 2026 Summit: GCCs Must Own Business Outcomes, Not Just Operations
exec.body: CGI hosted the second edition of CGI Elevate 2026 in Bengaluru, convening GCC heads and technology leaders to address GCC evolution in an AI-first landscape. Key discussions centred on shifting GCC mandates from cost-centre delivery to strategic outcome ownership, with AI serving as the primary enabler of this transformation. Industry speakers stressed that GCCs lacking P&L accountability risk losing influence to product and platform teams.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: The industry is consolidating around the view that GCCs must hold outcome accountability—leaders who have not yet restructured mandates from service delivery to business ownership are at risk of organisational marginalisation.

exec.title: KPMG-AMCHAM Report: Pune Now Hosts 500+ GCCs from 30 Countries
exec.body: KPMG India and AMCHAM released a whitepaper establishing Pune as a major GCC growth frontier, citing 500+ centers from over 30 countries attracted by strong engineering talent, Maharashtra's GCC Policy 2025, and improving digital infrastructure. Maharashtra targets 400 new GCCs by 2030 with an estimated investment of ₹50,600 crore. The report highlights a significant US-firm presence and growing investments in AI and innovation as primary growth catalysts.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders evaluating India site strategy should now treat Pune as a tier-1 destination—state policy support, talent depth, and cost advantages versus Bengaluru make it especially compelling for engineering-heavy and BFSI mandates.

exec.title: India's GCCs at Most Critical Inflection Point in 25 Years: Expert
exec.body: Abhishek Rungta, CEO of Indus Net Technologies, argues India's 2,117-GCC ecosystem—generating $98.4 billion in FY26 revenue—has reached its most consequential transformation moment in a quarter century. GCCs built for cost efficiency and execution must now transition to owning AI adoption, process redesign, and enterprise decision authority. The next decade will reward centers that demonstrate strategic outcome ownership rather than operational delivery excellence.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: GCC heads must urgently audit the gap between their current delivery mandate and what global HQs will require in 2027—board-level commitment to elevating the GCC's strategic role is essential to retaining talent, investment, and global relevance.

themes.title: AI Reshapes India's Media GCCs as GenAI and Agentic AI Investments Scale
themes.body: An EY report on India's media and entertainment GCC ecosystem finds 83% of GCCs are investing in GenAI and 58% in agentic AI, with AI-linked roles rising from 21% to 44% of media GCC headcount between 2024 and 2025. Demand for GenAI specialists has surged 300% year-over-year, but a nearly 50% talent gap in GenAI and LLM skills persists. India's GCCs are transitioning from content support operations to AI-engineering, platform engineering, and advertising-technology hubs.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders in media, retail, and marketing-adjacent sectors must accelerate GenAI capability-building now—the talent gap creates a first-mover advantage for organisations that invest in structured AI upskilling programmes rather than competing for scarce external hires.

themes.title: EY: India's GCCs Leading the Shift to Intelligent AI-Native Enterprises
themes.body: EY's latest analysis finds India's GCCs are driving enterprise-wide transition to AI-native architectures, with over 1,200 GCCs having embedded AI and ML capabilities out of 2,117 total centers. GCCs are deploying GenAI most actively in customer service (65%), finance (53%), and operations (49%), with 71% making reskilling a core talent strategy pillar. The shift from cost-centre to intelligence hub is accelerating as GCCs take ownership of global products, platforms, and business outcomes.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders who have not yet built a formal AI-native transformation roadmap risk falling behind peer organisations—embedding AI in core workflows, not just pilot programmes, is now a baseline expectation from global headquarters.

themes.title: Google's Agentic AI Repositioning Puts India's GCCs Under Competitive Pressure
themes.body: Following Google Cloud Next 2026, analysts warn that Google's strategic pivot toward agentic AI—autonomous agents capable of multi-step enterprise workflows—represents a structural threat and opportunity for India's GCCs simultaneously. GCCs that fail to redesign workflows around agentic systems risk having automation replace significant portions of their current delivery scope. Organisations that adopt agent-based operations first stand to compress cycle times dramatically and shift headcount toward higher-order strategic work.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: Agentic AI is not a future-state scenario—it is being deployed in enterprise production systems now; GCC leaders should immediately identify the 24% of tasks fully automatable and 42% augmentable in their centres and build implementation plans with defined timelines.

themes.title: Agentic AI Enterprise Adoption Hits 72% Production Globally; 60% Governance Gap Remains
themes.body: A new report from the Agentic AI Institute reveals that 72% of enterprises globally have moved agentic AI deployments from pilot to production, signalling mainstream adoption. However, a critical 60% governance gap remains, with most organisations lacking robust frameworks for agent oversight, accountability, and risk management. Finance and Customer Experience functions within GCCs are identified as the highest-value deployment areas for agentic AI solutions.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: India's GCC leaders face a dual mandate—accelerate agentic AI deployment to match enterprise peers while building governance frameworks proactively; failure to govern agent deployments creates operational, compliance, and reputational risks that headquarters will hold GCC leadership accountable for.

themes.title: EY AIdea of India 2026: Agentic AI to Deliver 41.7% Productivity Gain by 2027
themes.body: EY's AIdea of India 2026 report outlines how India's GCCs are designing AI-first workforces through a hybrid model pairing human expertise with autonomous AI agents. Salesforce research projects Indian HR leaders expect agentic AI adoption to jump 383% by 2027, delivering a 41.7% productivity gain. GCCs that combine agent deployment with structured human-AI collaboration frameworks are achieving the greatest productivity uplift, with 44% of GCC professionals already using AI as a core part of their daily work.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: Workforce strategy is inseparable from AI strategy—GCC leaders must redesign roles, reward systems, and talent pipelines around human-AI collaboration rather than treating AI as a tooling addition to existing job descriptions.

deals.title: T-Mobile Opens Hyderabad GCC in 250,000 Sq Ft Facility, Targets 1,000 Employees
deals.body: US telecom giant T-Mobile launched its Global Capability Center in Hyderabad's Hitec City, operating from a 250,000-square-foot facility through its TMUS Global Solutions entity, with a target of nearly 1,000 professionals by 2027. The centre will build capabilities across software engineering, DevOps, product development, data analytics, cybersecurity, and AI-driven digital transformation. T-Mobile's Hyderabad entry underscores the city's accelerating momentum as a GCC destination, with 41% of new GCC setups in 2025-2026 choosing Hyderabad over Bengaluru.
deals.why: Strategic Implication: T-Mobile's large-scale Hyderabad commitment validates the city's tier-1 status for technology GCCs—existing GCC leaders in Hyderabad should expect intensified talent competition, while those considering Hyderabad expansion should accelerate hiring pipelines before the market tightens further.

deals.title: UP, Gujarat and Andhra Pradesh Compete for Media GCC Capital Beyond Bengaluru
deals.body: Uttar Pradesh, Gujarat, and Andhra Pradesh are positioning themselves as contenders for media and entertainment GCC investments traditionally dominated by Bengaluru, Hyderabad, and Mumbai, per an EY-FICCI report. State governments are offering land waivers, capital subsidies, and talent support schemes to attract global media firms establishing content operations, platform engineering, and ad-tech centres. India has emerged as the preferred destination for global media companies across broadcasting, streaming, gaming, and AI-led content workflows.
deals.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders planning Indian expansion or satellite offices should actively evaluate emerging state incentive packages—the combination of lower costs, state subsidies, and growing talent pools in UP, Gujarat, and AP can significantly reduce the total cost of establishment versus metro locations.

deals.title: Hyderabad Tops India GCC Rankings with 41% of New Setups, Overtaking Bengaluru
deals.body: Hyderabad has overtaken Bengaluru as India's leading destination for new GCC launches, capturing 41-46% of all new setups in 2025-2026, with 41+ of roughly 95 new GCCs choosing the city versus 21 for Bengaluru. Operating costs for real estate and talent in Hyderabad remain 15-20% lower than Bengaluru, while Telangana's pro-GCC policy creates a compelling entry proposition. Within the first two months of 2026 alone, three major GCCs launched in Hyderabad, confirming the city's acceleration.
deals.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders evaluating new delivery locations should model Hyderabad at the top of their evaluation matrix—the cost arbitrage, talent availability, and policy support now rival any city in India for technology and engineering mandates.

risks.title: India GCC AI Talent Deficit at 53%; GenAI Attrition Spikes to 25-30% Annually
risks.body: Zinnov's 2026 GCC salary and attrition report finds India's AI talent deficit stands at 53%—meaning fewer than one qualified candidate exists for every two open AI/ML GCC roles. GenAI and LLM engineer attrition is spiking to 25-30% annually, creating a per-engineer replacement cost of ₹25-50 lakh. With 500,000 gross GCC hires projected in 2026 and AI/ML skills at the top of demand lists, the talent war for AI capabilities is intensifying significantly.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders must treat internal AI talent development as a strategic priority—relying solely on external recruitment for GenAI roles is financially unsustainable given the 1.7x salary premium and high attrition; structured reskilling programmes now yield faster and higher-retention results.

risks.title: DPDPA 2026: GCCs Face ₹250 Crore Penalties as Compliance Deadlines Approach
risks.body: India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act rules, notified in November 2025, impose phased compliance obligations on all GCCs processing personal data of Indian individuals—including a Consent Manager Framework operational by November 2026 and full substantive compliance by May 2027. Penalties for failure to maintain security safeguards reach ₹250 crore (~$30 million) per violation, with data breach non-notification attracting ₹200 crore fines. GCCs must additionally maintain at least one serving copy of personal data on India-based servers, complicating cross-border data architectures.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCCs must initiate DPDPA compliance programmes immediately—the 18-month timeline to May 2027 is shorter than most enterprise compliance transformation cycles, and data localisation requirements will require infrastructure decisions that take months to implement.

risks.title: GCC Talent War Intensifies: Niche AI and Cloud Skills Commanding 1.7x Salary Premium
risks.body: India's GCC talent market is entering a bifurcated phase where niche AI, cloud, and cybersecurity skills command salary premiums of 1.7x over adjacent engineering roles, while general IT attrition stabilises at 14-18% annually. By end-2026, one in four GCC roles is projected to be contractual, reflecting a structural shift in workforce composition. The pressure is most acute in Bengaluru, where wage inflation is pricing out smaller GCCs unable to match hyperscaler compensation benchmarks.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCC heads should proactively adjust compensation bands for high-risk skill categories now rather than reactively responding to attrition—the cost of retention is materially lower than replacement, and establishing internal talent marketplaces can reduce dependency on external premium hires.

risks.title: Tier-2 Cities Offer GCCs 25-30% Lower Talent Costs with Growing AI Talent Pools
risks.body: India's tier-2 GCC expansion is accelerating, with cities like Ahmedabad, Jaipur, Indore, Coimbatore, and Kochi offering 25-30% lower talent costs and 50% real estate savings versus tier-1 metros. A 30-40% increase in demand for GCCs in tier-2 cities is expected in the near term, driven by improved digital infrastructure, state GCC policies, and hybrid working models. Tier-2 cities are proving especially strong for finance, analytics, operations, and IT shared services functions.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders can materially reduce cost-per-hire and improve retention by establishing satellite delivery units in tier-2 cities—this distributed model also reduces concentration risk and broadens the talent pipeline beyond saturated metro markets.

risks.title: Union Budget 2026 Extends Cloud Tax Holiday to 2047, Raises Safe Harbour to ₹2,000 Crore
risks.body: India's Union Budget 2026-27 introduced significant GCC-friendly policy reforms: extension of tax holidays for cloud and data-centre services within SEZs until 2047, a safe harbour margin increase from ₹300 crore to ₹2,000 crore for IT services, and a transfer pricing certainty framework for long-term GCC investments. SEZ amendments also relaxed land norms for semiconductor and electronics GCCs. These measures directly reduce the cost of operating enterprise-scale cloud and AI infrastructure in India.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders investing in AI infrastructure and cloud platforms should accelerate those investments now to lock in the extended tax holiday regime—the policy stability signal from Budget 2026 also supports the business case for expanding GCC mandates to include data centre ownership and AI platform operations.

risks.title: India GCC Ecosystem Hits $98.4B Revenue in FY26; 2,117 Centres Now Active
risks.body: The NASSCOM-Zinnov GCC Landscape Report 2026 documents India's 2,117 GCCs generating $98.4 billion in annual revenue—a 32% increase since FY21—with more than 1,200 centres having embedded AI and ML capabilities. Over 506 Forbes Global 2000 companies now run operations from India, and nearly half of all GCCs established since FY21 were built with AI as a core focus from inception. The ecosystem employs 2.36 million professionals and is positioned to surpass $100 billion in revenue in FY27.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: India's GCC market is at a scale where it commands serious global strategic attention—GCC leaders should leverage these industry-level statistics in HQ conversations to secure expanded mandates, additional investment, and greater decision-making autonomy.

competitor.title: Persistent Systems Appoints Senior VP as GCC Head for BFSI Segment
competitor.body: Persistent Systems appointed Srijita Sinha as Senior Vice President and GCC Head for BFSI, reinforcing its strategic focus on Banking, Financial Services, and Insurance GCCs increasingly functioning as global innovation hubs. Sinha brings 27+ years of experience across Infosys and Wipro—where she served as GM and Sales Leader for BFSI—and will lead Persistent's efforts to deepen BFSI GCC partnerships. The appointment signals a targeted leadership investment to capture the growing BFSI GCC segment, where AI, risk analytics, and regulatory technology capabilities are in high demand.
competitor.why: Strategic Implication: Persistent's dedicated BFSI GCC leadership hire signals how IT services firms are structuring around GCC wallet share—BFSI GCC leaders should expect more specialised, capability-focused competition from services partners for both talent and technology contracts.

competitor.title: India GCCs Outpace IT Services Firms in AI and Cloud Talent Acquisition
competitor.body: TechRepublic reports that India-based GCCs are outpacing traditional IT services firms in AI and cloud talent acquisition across APAC, with mid-to-senior hires rising from 60% to over 77% of GCC hiring between 2023 and FY26. GCCs are hiring fastest in AI/ML engineering (especially LLMs and GenAI), cloud platform engineering, cybersecurity, and product management. The hiring gap versus IT services firms has widened as GCCs offer clearer ownership mandates and higher compensation for strategic roles.
competitor.why: Strategic Implication: GCCs now have a distinct talent brand advantage over traditional IT services firms for senior AI and cloud professionals—leaders should actively leverage this in employer branding, emphasising outcome ownership, global mandates, and technology leadership in hiring campaigns.

competitor.title: India's GCC Revolution: Delivery Engines Evolving into Enterprise Nerve Centres
competitor.body: IBTimes India reports India's GCC ecosystem is undergoing structural transformation, with centres evolving from back-office delivery hubs into strategic nerve centres that architect enterprise-wide technology decisions. The 2,117 GCCs active in FY26 generated $98.4 billion in revenue—a 32% increase since FY21—with nearly half of all GCCs established since FY2021 built with AI as a core focus from day one. GCCs are now accountable for global product ownership, R&D, platform architecture, and enterprise-level business outcomes.
competitor.why: Strategic Implication: The narrative gap between GCCs as delivery engines and GCCs as innovation leaders is closing rapidly—leaders must ensure their centre's mandate, internal branding, and global stakeholder perception reflect this evolved identity to attract the talent and budget required for the next phase.

competitor.title: India's Media GCCs Evolve into AI, Engineering and Content Hubs: EY-FICCI Report
competitor.body: India's media and entertainment GCCs are taking on increasingly strategic roles across AI engineering, content operations, and platform monetisation, according to an EY-FICCI report. GCCs are moving beyond back-office support to own core business capabilities across broadcasting, streaming, gaming, advertising, and content production. The AI-to-human workforce ratio is shifting measurably, with AI-linked jobs rising from 21% to 44% of media GCC headcount in two years.
competitor.why: Strategic Implication: The convergence of AI and creative capabilities in India's GCCs represents a new value proposition for global media and consumer companies—GCC leaders in adjacent sectors should evaluate whether their centres can similarly expand into AI-driven creative and platform roles to deepen global mandate ownership.

talent.title: GCCs Pivot from Volume Hiring to Targeted AI and Cybersecurity Capability Acquisition
talent.body: Experts quoted in Economic Times report a decisive pivot in India's GCC talent strategy—from volume-based hiring to targeted capability acquisition in AI, cybersecurity, cloud, and data engineering. After adding over 150,000 net headcount in 2025, GCCs are projected to add 200,000 more in 2026, but with gross hiring approaching 500,000 annually when replacement is factored in, quality-over-quantity becomes imperative. The strongest demand is concentrated in GenAI engineers, MLOps specialists, cloud architects, cybersecurity professionals, and product managers.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: GCC talent leaders who continue to optimise for headcount velocity risk building organisations with the wrong skill mix—the shift to AI-augmented operations requires a deliberate re-weighting of hiring criteria toward deep capability roles even at higher per-head cost.

talent.title: 81% of India GCCs Upskilling on GenAI; Reskilling Now Shapes Core Talent Strategy
talent.body: NASSCOM data shows 81% of Indian GCCs are actively upskilling internal teams on GenAI, while 66% prioritise deep domain expertise alongside technical skills, and 71% have made reskilling a core pillar of their talent strategy. The shift reflects a calculation that internal reskilling produces role-ready AI talent faster and with 2x higher retention compared to external recruitment, where the talent pipeline for niche AI roles takes 3-6 months and commands 30-60% pay premiums. With 53% of GCC AI roles unfilled and GenAI demand up 300% year-over-year, the external market cannot meet demand alone.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: GCC talent leaders should establish formal GenAI upskilling academies now—organisations that build internal AI capability pipelines will gain a structural talent cost and retention advantage over those relying on the external market.

talent.title: India Skills Report 2026: Employability Reaches 56.35% as Nation Emerges as Global Talent Hub
talent.body: India's 2026 Skills Report shows overall graduate employability reaching 56.35%, with AI tools, digital literacy, and gig-economy readiness emerging as the defining differentiators. India is projected to produce 1 million AI-skilled professionals by end-2026, though a 53% deficit versus actual AI job demand persists. GCCs are increasingly partnering with engineering colleges and IITs to build campus-to-capability pipelines that bypass the external talent market for emerging roles.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders should establish structured campus hiring and early-career AI apprenticeship programmes now—organisations that build brand equity with India's top engineering institutions will access AI talent before it enters the open market and at a fraction of the lateral hiring cost.

talent.title: 2026 GCC Hiring Boom: 2,000+ Onboardings Per Working Day Projected
talent.body: India's GCC ecosystem is on track for approximately 500,000 gross hires in 2026—nearly 2,000 onboardings per working day—combining 200,000 net additions and approximately 300,000 replacement hires on a 2-million professional base. Mid-to-senior talent now represents over 77% of GCC hiring by volume, up from 60% in 2023, reflecting the shift to capability-led growth. AI/ML, platform engineering, cloud, and cybersecurity remain the highest-demand areas, with contractual roles projected to represent one-in-four positions by year-end.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: The volume of replacement hiring required means that reducing attrition by even 2-3 percentage points has outsized financial impact—GCC leaders should treat retention investment as a capital allocation decision, particularly for high-value AI and engineering roles.

policy.title: India DPDPA Rules: Consent Manager Framework Becomes Operational November 2026
policy.body: India's Digital Personal Data Protection Rules 2025, notified in November 2025, establish a phased compliance timeline placing the Consent Manager Framework operational by November 2026 and full substantive compliance—covering data breach notification, individual rights, and security safeguards—effective by May 2027. All GCCs processing personal data of Indian individuals must comply regardless of sector, including cross-border data flows. Fisher Phillips analysis notes that pre-checked consent and implied permissions are specifically prohibited, requiring GCCs to rebuild consent architectures.
policy.why: Strategic Implication: GCCs must launch DPDPA transformation programmes immediately—the November 2026 Consent Manager deadline requires technology infrastructure decisions in the next 90 days, and organisations that delay risk both compliance exposure and last-minute implementation costs.

policy.title: India Developing Federal GCC Policy with Fresh Incentives for New Setups
policy.body: India Briefing reports that the central government is developing a federal GCC policy framework to complement state-level schemes, with a focus on fresh incentives for new GCC setups, capability expansion, and R&D mandates. Current state-level policies across Karnataka, Telangana, Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, and Gujarat have created a fragmented incentive landscape; a federal policy would create uniform baseline benefits applicable across all GCC locations. The policy is expected to address transfer pricing clarity, safe harbour norms, and talent development subsidies.
policy.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders planning multi-site India expansion should monitor federal policy developments closely—a unified national framework could significantly simplify regulatory navigation across state boundaries and unlock new investment incentive structures not currently available.

policy.title: DPDP Act Data Localisation Requirement Complicates GCC Cross-Border Data Architecture
policy.body: India's DPDP Act requires all organisations—including GCCs—to maintain at least one serving copy of personal data on India-based servers, creating significant architectural implications for GCCs operating global data platforms. Glocert International's 2026 compliance overview notes penalties of up to ₹250 crore for security failures and ₹200 crore for breach non-notification represent material financial risk. Organisations must appoint a Data Protection Officer and conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments for high-risk processing activities.
policy.why: Strategic Implication: GCC CIOs and CTOs must immediately audit existing data flows to identify cross-border transfers that will require localisation—this has direct infrastructure, cloud architecture, and vendor contract implications that require multi-quarter lead time to resolve before the May 2027 compliance deadline.

policy.title: Union Budget 2026-27 SEZ Amendments Strengthen GCC Global Competitiveness
policy.body: India's Union Budget 2026-27 introduced SEZ amendments specifically designed to improve GCC cost structures and investment viability: minimum land requirements for semiconductor GCCs were relaxed, encumbrance norms were eased, and DTA supply permissions for semiconductor products were expanded. A special one-time measure permits manufacturing SEZ units to sell into the domestic market at concessional duty rates, and tax incentives for cloud and data-centre operations within SEZs were extended. These reforms aim to improve capacity utilisation and lower export costs for technology-focused GCCs.
policy.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders operating within or evaluating SEZ structures should work with legal and tax teams to model the full benefit impact of the 2026-27 amendments—the combination of relaxed land norms, DTA supply permissions, and tax holiday extensions may materially alter the financial case for SEZ versus non-SEZ operations.

tech.title: ANSR Survey: AI Driving Productivity Boost for 75% of GCC Professionals
tech.body: An ANSR-led survey of GCC professionals finds that 75% report measurable AI-driven productivity improvements in their day-to-day work, with the strongest gains in code generation, documentation, data analysis, and workflow automation. GCCs that have moved beyond AI pilots to systematic deployment across engineering and operations teams report 2-3x faster cycle times for core deliverables. The survey highlights that AI augmentation is shifting GCC professionals toward higher-order strategic tasks as routine work is automated.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: The productivity gains from enterprise AI are now empirically measurable at the GCC level—leaders should establish structured AI adoption metrics and baseline them now to demonstrate value to global headquarters and build the business case for further investment.

tech.title: NVIDIA Powers India AI Mission with Shakti Cloud: 20,000+ Blackwell Ultra GPUs
tech.body: NVIDIA is powering India's national AI infrastructure initiative through Yotta's Shakti Cloud, built on 20,000+ NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra GPUs across campuses in Navi Mumbai and Greater Noida, delivering pay-per-use GPU-dense AI cloud services. India's broader AI mission is backed by government investment in sovereign AI infrastructure, creating enterprise-grade compute accessible to GCCs for large-scale model training and inference workloads. The infrastructure is designed to make India-based GCCs competitive with global peers on AI development speed and scale.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: India-based GCC leaders undertaking large-scale AI development should evaluate sovereign cloud infrastructure as a complement to hyperscaler services—access to Blackwell-class compute at competitive rates removes a key bottleneck that previously required workloads to be run offshore.

tech.title: EY: Agentic AI GCCs Are Reshaping Enterprise Operating Models Across Finance and CX
tech.body: EY's analysis finds that agentic AI is driving measurable transformation in GCC operating models across finance, supply chain, and customer operations—with 24% of enterprise tasks fully automatable and 42% significantly augmentable. GCCs deploying hybrid agentic models—pairing autonomous AI agents with human oversight—are reporting faster forecasting, reduced manual effort, and compressed cycle times as structural outcomes. Finance and Customer Experience are identified as the highest-value deployment areas, with EY noting that governance readiness is required before scaling agentic deployments.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: GCC operations heads should map their function-level task inventory against agentic AI automation potential—the 66% combined automation and augmentation opportunity represents the largest structural productivity lever available to GCC leaders in 2026.

tech.title: Deloitte 2026 Enterprise AI Report: India Leads Global AI Adoption at 40%
tech.body: Deloitte's State of AI in the Enterprise 2026 report finds India is the global leader in enterprise AI adoption, with 40% of Indian organisations reporting significant or full AI deployment versus a 28% global average. Indian enterprises rank AI first out of 15 countries for use in strategic decision-making, reflecting GCCs' growing role as AI centres of excellence. The report identifies governance, talent, and change management as the three primary barriers preventing organisations from scaling from AI pilots to enterprise-wide deployment.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: India's leadership in enterprise AI adoption creates a tangible GCC differentiator—leaders should leverage these statistics in HQ conversations to secure expanded mandates, while simultaneously investing in AI governance frameworks to sustain the leadership position.

tech.title: India's GenAI Unicorn Krutrim Shifts to Cloud Services as AI Model Economics Evolve
tech.body: Krutrim, India's first GenAI unicorn, has pivoted from AI model development to cloud services following a business overhaul in late 2025, reflecting the capital-intensive reality of competing with global foundation model providers. The shift signals a maturation in India's enterprise AI ecosystem, where GCCs are increasingly consuming AI services from established cloud and model providers rather than building proprietary models. This consolidation creates cleaner procurement pathways for GCC teams integrating AI into enterprise workflows.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: GCC technology leaders should leverage the maturing India AI vendor landscape to accelerate AI platform adoption through cloud-native services rather than custom model development—this reduces time-to-deployment, lowers capital investment, and allows GCC engineering teams to focus on integration and business logic rather than model infrastructure.
